title: Escatologia face ravagii în Statele Unite și în Rusia
summary: The article analyzes how eschatological (end-times) thinking has deeply influenced both American and Russian political ideology. It traces the history of dispensationalism in the US from the 19th century to its modern cultural and political influence, arguing that 55% of American adults believe in Christ's return and that this apocalyptic mindset fuels conspiracy theories and the MAGA movement. The author draws parallels between American evangelical eschatology and Russian ideological currents, suggesting both superpowers are driven by messianic visions.
talking_points: Dispensationalism, though declining as theology, remains a dominant cultural and political force in America with 55% of adults believing in Christ's return
The collapse of structured dispensationalist doctrine has spawned fragmented apocalyptic movements that feed conspiracy theories and the MAGA movement
Aleksandr Dugin identified American messianic fanaticism as a hidden driver of US policy, comparing it to Islamism, communism, and Nazism
George W. Bush reportedly told Jacques Chirac in 2003 that 'Gog and Magog are at work in the Middle East' while seeking French support for the Iraq war
Evangelical Christians' belief that the world is irredeemably lost leads to political indifference on issues like climate change
author: Francoise Thom
